Aerodynamic force is the reaction force of airflow on the missile.
Aerodynamic force is a complex distributed force system related to many factors such as missile speed, the relative orientation between airflow and the body, and altitude.
The center of pressure is the point at which the distributed force system acting on the missile is equivalent to a resultant force according to the force-system simplification principle.
The center of pressure is not the same as the aerodynamic center; the aerodynamic center is the point of action of the resultant lift caused only by a nonzero angle of attack.

Aerodynamic coefficients measured in the wind tunnel are obtained in the body coordinate system. To obtain aerodynamic forces in the velocity coordinate system, the corresponding coordinate transformation matrix is required.
With the aerodynamic layout and geometric dimensions given, the lift of the body depends only on angle of attack and elevator deflection.
Lateral stability refers to the ability of the body to automatically return to equilibrium after a temporary external disturbance moment causes it to tilt about the longitudinal axis oX1.
Civil aircraft have dihedral angle and sweep angle that raise the wings in order to enhance lateral stability.
Whether the body is statically stable depends only on its geometric layout and mass distribution!
If the body is statically stable, the partial derivative of pitching moment coefficient with respect to angle of attack is greater than zero!
As long as the body is statically stable, it can certainly provide a restoring moment!
All uncontrolled flight vehicles such as rockets and reentry warheads must be statically stable!
The main factors affecting the yawing moment of the body are sideslip angle, rudder deflection, and their partial derivatives
Directional static stability also depends only on geometric layout!
Hinge moment is the moment of aerodynamic force on the control surface about the hinge axis and is unrelated to the output torque of the actuator!
The output torque of the actuator should be at least greater than the hinge moment; when selecting an actuator it is generally taken as twice the hinge moment.

Inertial navigation is a basic autonomous navigation mode. It depends on the accuracy of inertial devices such as accelerometers measuring the acceleration of the body center of mass and attitude gyros measuring attitude or angular rate, and it is independent of the outside world!
Another major error source of strapdown navigation is the initial alignment error.
Satellite navigation and positioning can eliminate accumulated errors caused by gyro drift in inertial navigation, so an inertial navigation device is not necessary.
If the output torque of the actuator is larger, the bandwidth becomes narrower and the response becomes slower.

If the wing is designed such that its leading edge is behind the Mach line, the Mach angle is smaller than the leading-edge angle; this is called a subsonic leading edge, and wave drag does not exist.
For surface-to-air missiles, wing area depends on the required maneuver overload; for subsonic cruise missiles, wing area depends on the required lift-to-drag ratio.
The determination of wing size is based on the designed wing area and preselected geometric parameters such as aspect ratio, taper ratio, and sweep angle.

After the geometric layout is selected, the design of aerodynamic control surfaces depends on the required control efficiency, namely the control-to-stability ratio, and the hinge moment of the control surface.
Generally, subsonic flight vehicles have wings with larger aspect ratio, while supersonic flight vehicles have smaller aspect ratio, less than 2
The maximum lift-to-drag ratio of a missile is proportional to the square root of lift-curve slope and inversely proportional to the square root of the zero-lift drag coefficient.
Wing sweep can effectively reduce wave drag during supersonic flight.
The total normal-force coefficient of the body is approximately the sum of the normal-force coefficients of the fuselage, wing, and tail.
The static stability margin of a missile is related not only to geometric design but also to mass distribution, and it also changes with flight time.
In missile design, the tail area is usually designed last to satisfy the static stability margin requirement.
The static stability margins of the X-shaped layout and the + shaped layout are equal.
Although missile body dynamics are nonlinear, engineering tests show that piecewise linearization can be used with classical linearization theory to study and design missile control stability.
The lower the launch speed of an uncontrolled body, the more severe the adverse effect of gravity on flight stability.
An uncontrolled body must be statically stable in order to be dynamically stable.
Because a sounding rocket is uncontrolled, its geometric design must not only be statically stable, but must also provide sufficient restoring moment to resist disturbance moments such as wind shear.
The stability of disturbed body motion can be considered using only the third-order short-period perturbation motion.

The control-to-stability ratio is related to the operating point (for example, the current trim angle of attack and control-surface deflection), and also to the slopes of the moment coefficients at that operating point (such as the partial derivative of pitching-moment coefficient with respect to angle of attack and the partial derivative of control moment coefficient with respect to control-surface deflection).

The control-to-stability ratio is the ratio of the increment of trim angle of attack to the increment of control-surface deflection, and it must be calculated according to the aerodynamic-coefficient table and the incremental moment-coefficient balance equation.
Linearity analysis means giving the function curve between a certain moment and the corresponding independent variable at a given operating point and analyzing the degree of linear proportionality.
Because the lateral size is small, missiles usually do not have dedicated ailerons in order to save space and reduce weight.
The deflection of each control surface produces corresponding control moments about the three axes of the body.
The allocation matrix from three equivalent control-surface deflections to four actual control-surface deflections is not unique.
Flight stability of a statically unstable body must rely on negative-feedback control to create artificial damping.
The six-component aerodynamic data table quantitatively reflects the aerodynamic characteristics jointly caused by geometric design and component arrangement.
Controlling a statically unstable body is relatively more difficult than controlling a statically stable body, and it also places higher requirements on actuator output torque and bandwidth.

In component arrangement, the seeker is usually placed in the radome at the front end of the fuselage, followed by the warhead and instrument compartment (including navigation and electronic equipment), and finally the engine.

The nose shape and fineness ratio of the fuselage, the length and diameter of the cylindrical section, and the shapes, areas, and installation positions of the wing and tail affect not only lift-to-drag ratio and maneuver overload, but also stability.

In addition to ensuring the functional integration of components, component arrangement should also consider mass layout so that the body coordinate axes are principal inertia axes and the inertia tensor is diagonal as much as possible.
When selecting an engine scheme in overall design, existing mature technology is always preferred, or technology requiring only minor modification.
The main difference between a rocket engine and an air-breathing jet engine is that the former carries oxidizer, while the latter instead inhales air.
After startup of a turbojet engine, the turbine working cycle is driven by gas from the combustion chamber.
A. ramjet has no compressor, turbine, or other rotor components; compression is achieved by inlet ram pressure.
A. ramjet cannot be started directly and requires a booster to accelerate it to about Mach 2.5

Typical tactical missile engines (such as those for anti-tank missiles and surface-to-air missiles) are often divided into a booster stage and a sustainer stage, or use a single chamber with dual thrust. The booster stage has high thrust and short duration, while the sustainer stage has lower thrust but longer operating time.

According to where the guidance command is formed, remote control guidance is divided into remote command guidance and beam-riding guidance.
The transformation matrix between the body coordinate system and the ground coordinate system can be obtained through two rotations.
Pitching moment, yawing moment, and rolling moment are moments of aerodynamic force relative to the missile center of mass.
According to the right-hand rule, the moment causing the missile to yaw to the left is a positive yawing moment.
To ensure that the missile flies along the predetermined trajectory, the required overload should be greater than the available overload.
When using the parallel-approach guidance method, the required normal overload of the missile is greater than the normal overload of the target.
Pursuit guidance is a method in which the missile axis or nose direction points toward the target.
Proportional navigation has the characteristics of a trajectory that curves early and straightens later, terminal overload that is easily affected, and wide engineering application.
Both the pursuit method and the three-point method are special forms of proportional navigation.
When using the three-point guidance method, the missile is located on the line connecting the target and the guidance station.
The trajectory characteristics of the three-point method are a relatively straight trajectory, dynamic errors that are difficult to compensate, and trajectory sagging.

Radio homing guidance is a guidance method in which equipment installed on the missile receives radio waves radiated or reflected by the target, tracks the target, forms guidance commands, and guides the missile toward the target.
Missiles using active radio homing guidance systems are usually also called anti-radiation missiles.

The RF amplifier in the radar seeker receiving system, also called the front-end microwave assembly, directly amplifies the received echo signal while reducing its own noise; its main function is to improve receiver sensitivity.

The primary task of the radar seeker information-processing system is to detect target signals from complex interfered signals and extract relative missile-target position and motion parameters in real time to obtain guidance information.
The preset loop is a typical speed control system. Structurally, it consists of two basically identical and mutually independent azimuth and pitch loops.
The function of the seeker angle-tracking loop is to make the radar seeker echo antenna automatically track the target in angle, so that the seeker can continuously receive the target echo signal.
The wavelength of the infrared radiation energy of an object is proportional to its temperature.
Due to the selective absorption of the atmosphere, the energy of infrared radiation is concentrated near certain specific wavelength bands.
The image-point position reflects the magnitude and direction of the target deviation from the optical axis, and there is a one-to-one correspondence between the target and the image point.
In modulation-disk design, the equal-transmittance principle can eliminate the influence of large-area background radiation on target-signal extraction.
Rigidity in space and precession are inherent properties of a gyroscope, whether the gyroscope is operating or not.
Common optical gyroscopes include laser gyroscopes and fiber-optic gyroscopes.

In general, any two coordinate systems in space can be made to coincide through three rotations. Each rotation yields an attitude transformation matrix, and right-multiplying these three matrices in sequence gives the overall attitude transformation matrix.

The purpose of using composite guidance is to increase the operating range of the guidance system, overcome the shortcomings of a single guidance method, exploit the advantages of various guidance methods, and improve anti-interference performance and guidance accuracy.

After the allowable-intercept flag is formed, the control algorithm uses missile-target motion parameters calculated in the midcourse guidance stage to provide the target's relative distance, velocity, and acceleration information with respect to the missile, and the seeker searches according to the target's approximate azimuth.
From a technical perspective, the principle followed by a multimode composite scheme is that the operating frequencies of the modes should be as close as possible in the electromagnetic spectrum.

A. missile is a space-moving body. In addition to the six states of center-of-mass motion and rotation about the center of mass, the body may also have elastic vibration, liquid sloshing when carrying liquid propellant, and engine nozzle swing under thrust-vector control. The final dynamic performance of the body is a combination of these motions.

After writing the missile motion differential equations by the small-perturbation method, higher-order terms above the second order of these small increments can be neglected, so the motion differential equations with perturbation quantities as basic motion variables become constant-coefficient differential equations.

From the perspective of maintaining and changing missile attitude, missile stability and control are contradictory; but from the perspective of ensuring that the missile flies along the reference trajectory, they are consistent.

The control-surface loop contains a tachogenerator and a position sensor. The tachogenerator measures the angular velocity of control-surface deflection and feeds it back to the amplifier, so that a certain control signal corresponds to a certain control-surface deflection.
The forces usually acting on a missile can be expressed as the resultant of gravity, engine thrust, and aerodynamic force.

In a rectangular-coordinate control system, the missile control force consists of two mutually perpendicular components. The guidance subsystem in the guidance equipment measures deviations along the two coordinate axes to form two control signals, one in the pitch direction and one in the roll direction.
The time constant of an aerodynamic control surface is larger than that of a direct-force lateral jet device, so aerodynamic control can achieve higher control accuracy than direct-force control.
The performance of a thrust-vector control system can be described from four aspects: jet deflection angle, lateral force coefficient, axial thrust loss, and driving force.
